speaker: interviewer
text: Tape number five. Survivor and war crimes trials participant is Lawrence Rhee, and the data is April 9th, 1996. We left off on the last tape, I asked you if you can remember any other memorable incidents that took place either inside or outside of the courtroom personally. And you mentioned that you got some pictures [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] kind of off the cuff. And I wanted to ask you more about that.

speaker: interviewee
text: All right.

speaker: interviewer
text: What exactly did you do? Did you actually shoot pictures inside the courtroom?

speaker: interviewee
text: I actually shot pictures inside the courtroom for my own [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] enjoyment and fun or recollection, memory, memorabilia. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] But oftentimes I had to take the pictures, or most of the time, from the projection booth, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] from where I projected the films or the stills [INAUDIBLE]. And one of those pictures I took was of the defendants. And it so happened that you only can go to-- when you are in a closed room, you cannot get the angle you always want. So I took this particular picture of the defendants. And Goering is covered up, except for his belly sticking out, by one of the guards. And it was a pretty good picture otherwise.

speaker: interviewer
text: So through connections, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] as I mentioned before, I never went in there, had anything to do with the defendants. But the guard that guarded Hoffmann had the connection with the other guards, and he took this picture. I had it blown up. I blew it up.

speaker: interviewee
text: And they're all signed except-- autographed it, except Hess. But Goering, Schacht, and Franz Papen said to this guard that they didn't want to sign with the rest of the hoodlums on the same paper. They took out a little three by five card and wrote their names on it. And I pasted them to the back of the picture, so that in such a way that can be flipped up, and the names underneath are fully visible.

speaker: interviewer
text: So once again, who actually took the picture? It was your camera?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was my camera, and I took the picture.

speaker: interviewer
text: From where?

speaker: interviewee
text: From the projection booth.

speaker: interviewer
text: From the projection booth. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] Did your view of the defendants change in any way as the trial progressed?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know. I didn't agree with the judges to let Franz Papen go free. He was the, in my opinion, the person who engineered Hitler's foreign policy in there, by the taking over of the countries around Germany, of occupied Europe. Granted, he may not have had anything to do with the atrocities as such, but he was the direct cause that they were committed, by subjugating those. And he was just about the main diplomat who really engineered all these.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you talk a little bit more about Hoffman, Heinrich Hoffman? Because you did work so closely with him, and you said he was Hitler's personal photographer. Can you share anything else about him or any incidences with him? What did he look like?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, he was about my size, a little older, of course, than I am. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] He looked like a man on the street. I mean, his clothes were not the best, because he had to, as I mentioned before, he had the GI clothing. What would he look like? He was smart as a whip, intelligent.

speaker: interviewer
text: What kinds of conversations would you have with him? In your day-to-day workings with him, what would you talk about? And what did he talk about?

speaker: interviewee
text: He did not talk much about his association with Hitler, which I learned much later, in a book that I read just recently, was much closer than I had anticipated at that time. I had no way of knowing. Otherwise, I could have asked him, but I didn't know. But [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] he was smooth, real smooth. And we talked about this and that, but [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] nothing really, you know, world-shaking.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did he talk about his ideologies, his feelings, about Nazism? Did he ever discuss?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, we never discussed it. But he was one of the nine original party members. So that when the party was formed, way back in 1919, he was one of the original nine. And so he must have been a pretty staunch supporter.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did he talk about anti-Semitism at all to you?

speaker: interviewee
text: I never touched that field with him. And maybe I was too timid to ask. I don't know.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was he married, with a family?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, he had. In fact, one of the accused, Baldur von Schirach was his son-in-law. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And he was very tied up about his fate when his particular case came up. That much, I remember, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: But I don't know. Was he permitted to go to the courtroom then or not? I forget, I'll tell you frankly. What else did he say about Eva Braun? Did he talk at all about her?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, that's the only time I asked for her-- I asked him about her.

speaker: interviewer
text: And he knew her well?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, she was his employee. How well he knew her personally, I have no idea. But she was a pretty lady. I have a picture of hers with Hitler. I think that it's in on my table, the 50th birthday of Hitler. She's quite a pretty lady.

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened to Hoffmann? You said he was under arrest, Heinrich Hoffmann.

speaker: interviewee
text: He was under arrest by the Allied forces. And the Germans held a trial for him. It was a German trial. And they convicted him of war profiteering. He was tremendously rich. And what of his personal belongings he could keep later on, I don't know. But he was sentenced to 10 years of prison, of which he actually served five. And then he was released. I guess he was getting too old and feeble, that they couldn't-- but he has two sons, who were still alive in Germany and took over his business. And then just recently, in one of the magazines or papers, I read where they had a tremendous exhibition of Heinrich Hoffmann's pictures.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you ever have a real personal conversation? Did he ask you about your life? Or was there any kind of intimate conversation concerning anything like that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not really. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] He knew that I had been born in Germany, of course. My German gives that away. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] But not-- we didn't go too personal either at any time, as far as I can remember.

speaker: interviewer
text: So how far were they into the trial when you left? Were they almost at the end?

speaker: interviewee
text: They were nearing the end, yeah. Oh, now I remember the name. Fritzsche was the other guy, the German propaganda guy, that took Goebel's place. Fritzsche. It just came back to me.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you were near the end of the trial when you left?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, several weeks were left yet, but--

speaker: interviewer
text: Was there a moment in the time that you were there that, for you, was the most defining moment of the trial? Does anything stand out to you as the most defining moment? I know you weren't there for all of it, but did any one particular time stand out as a defining moment?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, as I mentioned before, when I was seated near this witness Ohlendorf, an SS General Ohlendorf, when he mentioned in the trials, as a witness, that more than five million Jews met the final solution, or the final solution was applied to more than five million Jews. That was a pretty-- [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] pretty hard-hitting moment.

speaker: interviewer
text: When exactly did you leave? What was the date?

speaker: interviewee
text: When did I what?

speaker: interviewer
text: When you left?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't remember. I have to look it up. I really don't remember.

speaker: interviewer
text: So after you were done, where did you go?

speaker: interviewee
text: I went by train to Bremerhaven, and I took a boat there, and back to the United States.

speaker: interviewer
text: And were you then discharged from the military?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I was already discharged.

speaker: interviewer
text: You were already discharged? It's while you were doing the trial you were discharged?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, I took my discharge.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then you were hired as a civilian?

speaker: interviewee
text: As a civilian.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you were paid as a civilian.

speaker: interviewee
text: As a civilian.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you came back to New Mexico, is that correct?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you speak about your experiences at Nuremberg with the community when you first came back?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, they all want-- everybody wanted to know, of course. And I showed my pictures to everybody who wanted to see them. And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I now take great delight, actual delight, in going to the schools, showing my documentary, which, for the schools, has been edited a little bit, to make it shorter, to make it fit into the time frame, and also to leave out some of the irrelevant talks that take place in there. But it gives me a great satisfaction to teach today's generation that these things are not inventions, that they actually happened. And when you show this picture, especially when the crowd is pretty harsh and awful, sudden this picture appears with a woman with her breast cut off, oh! [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] The whole crowd.

speaker: interviewer
text: What kinds of questions? Did you speak mainly to children?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, as long as their time permits. I have no time limit.

speaker: interviewer
text: What kinds of questions did the children ask you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Some are pretty relevant. Other ones are childish. But they ask me much how did you get these pictures? What are your personal feelings about it?

speaker: interviewee
text: Other ones are a little bit more philosophical. And what do I think should be done, could be done? And I say, what could be done is what I'm doing now, or should be done. Show you this.

speaker: interviewee
text: But I had an incident last year. It was really a highlight. I was showing this-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] well, I not only show, but I also give a lecture. And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] after it was all over, I was told by the teacher in charge that there were two German exchange students. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Excuse me. And I talked with them briefly after the lecture. And one of them said, you know, mister-- we were talking German. You are the first Jew I ever talked to. And I talked to the teacher, and I suggested to have these two and the teacher and her husband, who's also a teacher, come over for a cookout here. And we did. And in the meantime, I had copies made of the these, of my tape. And I gave the students the copy.

speaker: interviewer
text: The documentary you were referring to, what was that documentary that was done? You said there was a document done.

speaker: interviewee
text: That's the one you saw.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you tell me the name of it? And who did that?

speaker: interviewee
text: KNME-TV, Channel 5, here in Albuquerque.

speaker: interviewer
text: What are your attitudes or feelings about Holocaust denialists?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think they're the most unreasonable and far out persons that could ever live. I [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] think-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] they leave me speechless. How can they deny something like that, that so many of us have experienced?

speaker: interviewer
text: Have you been back to Nuremberg at all since the trials?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, about 13 years ago, my son was stationed in Germany in the Air Force. And he took us, Alicia and me, on a trip through Germany. And we spent just a couple of hours in Nuremberg.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was that like for you, going back?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was so rebuilt, I couldn't recognize it. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] It was so-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] But we also went, on that same trip, to a little place called Mittenwald, which is famous for the violins that are being built there. And it so happened that, during the war, we took that place, our regiment. And I was the one who interrupted the negotiation, the surrender negotiations, for that little place.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you satisfied with the verdicts reached for the defendants?

speaker: interviewee
text: With the exception of Franz Papen I think I was. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] The [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] rest of them-- Fritzsche I'm sure should not have been blamed. He shouldn't even have been in the trial, because he was a newcomer. He had only been in the job six weeks. You know, it's well-known that Goebbels was the former minister of propaganda. When he realized that the situation became untenable, he shot his wife and seven children and then shot himself. And Fritzsche was the one who took Goebbels' place. But I don't think he had anything to do with the-- and he was acquitted, and I felt that was correct.

speaker: interviewer
text: Are you aware that in 1950, a clemency board reviewed some of the Nuremberg sentences, resulting in some individual reductions?

speaker: interviewee
text: Right.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your reaction to this?

speaker: interviewee
text: I figured they were getting old enough, where they were harmless, really harmless, at the time. I mean, what. And just, for instance, I was against Hess being held in Spandau, where we had to have the pretty near military establishment just for him. I thought it useless. Not that his sentence wasn't appropriate, but I felt, at that time, it was a useless undertaking.

speaker: interviewer
text: When you returned to the United States, what career did you resume?

speaker: interviewee
text: Lumber. I went right back into the lumber business.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did you marry?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. In 1949, I had a chance to go to Mexico. I had an offer down there that paid me twice as much as I was actually receiving here. And I took it. And while in Mexico, I met my now wife, Alicia.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was her maiden name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Moreno. And in 1951, we got married, on the 4th of May. And it was quite a courtship. And we have four wonderful children, two boys and two girls.

speaker: interviewer
text: Their names?

speaker: interviewee
text: Norman Randolph, Robert Edward, Lillian, and Else Arlene. And they're all professionals and married to professionals. We're very lucky with-- lucky or mother's wisdom in bringing them up.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you remained in Mexico for how long?

speaker: interviewee
text: 10 Years.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then you came back to--

speaker: interviewee
text: We came back to the States, because the last job I held, it was the best job I ever held in my life. But the climate was of such a nature, that we feared for the health of the children. And this was a lumber camp that was way out in the sticks. And there was no doctor there, no school teacher. And my wife, even though she was a nurse anesthetist in her profession, in her profession-- but we did not particularly care, especially she, with the children, didn't particularly care to live in this place.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you went back to New Mexico?

speaker: interviewee
text: We went back to the company where I left from. Came right back.

speaker: interviewer
text: What, in your opinion, do you think should be the legacy of the Nuremberg trials?

speaker: interviewee
text: The legacy-- [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] I personally hope that nothing like that or even remotely approaching it should ever happen again. And the [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] lesson that I feel should be taken is that we should respect each other as human beings and not divide each other by racial or religious groups. And I think my wife, who is a staunch Catholic, and I am a good Jew, we will be married in June for 45 years. And I think [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] that proves my point.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you keep in touch with anyone that was present at the Nuremberg trials with you? Do you still keep  in touch?

speaker: interviewee
text: The only one here in town is Marvin Atlas, who was on the prosecution staff, who was a lawyer. But I did not know him during the trial days. I never had any contact with him. And he's about the only one. There is a lady, I told you before. Lizzie [INAUDIBLE] was a secretary, but whom I did not know.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you mentioned earlier, there was a reunion in Washington.

speaker: interviewee
text: In Washington.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was this reunion?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was quite a reunion of some of the details that came out, for instance, about Hitler's death, that I didn't know about; Goering's death, how did he get the capsule. And the doctor, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I think Dr. [? Lattimer, ?] told me-- or told us as a group, that Goering, while he was prisoner, always complained about chapped lips. And his skin was all red. He had a rash. And the guards became so used to his going to the other room, wherever it was, to get his [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] skin ointments, or whatever it was, that they didn't realize that that was the place where he kept his cyanide capsule. See, they had been bodily searched everywhere in the room, searched daily for cyanide, for the capsules. And that's where he kept it, and he dug it out of this jar. And that's how he, a few minutes before being, supposed to have been, hanged, got his jar out of there, bit it, and of course, died.

speaker: interviewer
text: When was this reunion?

speaker: interviewee
text: Just about two weeks ago.

speaker: interviewer
text: And it was for participants in the war crimes--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: --trials at Nuremberg?

speaker: interviewee
text: Another thing I learned that I hadn't known is that-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I always had thought that after Hitler married Eva Braun, that he gave orders to his troops that when he and Eva were to leave the bunker where there were, to shoot them and then douse their bodies with gasoline and burn them. But this same doctor told me something completely different. Hitler actually used a cyanide capsule, bit it, and the second he bit it, also fired a pistol into-- a shot into his head. So he had-- he wanted to make sure he'd die. And then I didn't ask how Eva Braun died, but the troops actually killed her or she committed suicide. But then the troops doused their bodies with gasoline. And the Russians were supposed to have picked up-- I mean, denied it for a long time. But they had picked up the bodies. And the reason they could identify the burned bodies, the Americans, later on, because they had dental x-rays of Hitler's. And they matched the skull x-rays with the x-rays they had.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you reflect back on your life now for a minute? You've been through so many experiences. Is there anything you would like to say before we finish this interview? How would you like to end it or sum it up?

speaker: interviewee
text: I wouldn't know what to change. I lived a happy and full and a very complete life, a wonderful family. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Was lucky. Maybe one thing I could-- would try to do-- I didn't have a chance while I was younger-- to go to college. But I barely finished high school in 1964, at night classes, when I had already my family. But that may be the only thing. But I am happy.

speaker: interviewer
text: Is there anything else you'd like to say?

speaker: interviewee
text: I would like to tell those people who look in or see this or see the evidence to be aware that racial and religious strife is one of the worst things that can happen to humanity. And we see it right now in Yugoslavia and have seen it in many other parts of the world since. And that's it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you very, very much.

speaker: interviewee
text: You're welcome.
